speaker: interviewer
text: --1995, And we're interviewing Leon Ginsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived here, and you said you straightened your pants. And they turned on the lights for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- it was nice. And then we ended up in this house, a big house, big room. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel here on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself. How did you get your ticket and your fare?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was through that organization. I came with a group, with an orphan group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come with a group of kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: With kids, yeah. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was interesting that some of the kids were kids that were running away from the Ukraine or from-- and you know, what always went through my mind is, you know, why are they running from-- the only time, you know, non-Jews ran away is because either they collaborated with the Germans-- in most cases.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you came here, and what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was put into a junior high school right away. And my first report card said across it, learning English, except a 95 in math. Somehow I had this math teacher--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Math teacher. He used to used to give me these side jobs. You know, he'd come over and say to me-- and I would add up, and he would double check it, probably, and there were no mistakes. He says-- He would say to me, you know, see, none of them could do that without a mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living? With whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my aunt, my grandfather's sister and husband. They were, of course, in their 70s. You know, so it's an older couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they already here--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were here before.

speaker: interviewer
text: --In the States the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. They came here before the war, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they were like a poor family. He was like working in a factory, some jobs, still working. And my aunt was very nice, except she wanted me to go to a yeshiva. And I wanted to please her in a lot of ways, but that I said, no. I want to go to a regular school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ended up going to public school. And then, within like one year, I graduated junior high school. And they-- my records went into some high school. And I decided I wanted to go to Brooklyn Technical High School. And the assistant principal who spoke Yiddish, he used to talk me. He said, said no, that's not for you. You know. He didn't want to insult me, saying, it's difficult or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I didn't know that it was a difficult school to get into. And I insisted, no, I want to go there. So I think he tried to humor me. And I missed the entrance exam. But there was a makeup exam. So he figured, OK, I'll let him swim. So he gave me a, a paper to go and take the makeup exam I took the makeup exam. And I must have done all right in the math, but I-- no way could I have done well on the English, because I didn't know English yet that well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they graded it right away. And maybe based on the fact that I-- that I'm just in the country about a year, they decided to accept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back with this paper. You know, I didn't know what it was, even. And I give it to the principal. He looks at me. He didn't believe it. So I went there. And then, I didn't think I would, would be able to go to college. So I took an electrical course. In fact, they had an option there with like-- of broadcasting. So I took that. I figured I was going to work in-- maybe in broadcasting. But then I got accepted to City College on my average. So then I said to myself, if I don't make it, I don't mind. I'll give it a try. So I gave it a try one semester. And then, during the recess, I found a job in a factory. So I was making some money. So I figured, I think I can manage it. Because my aunt, they couldn't really help me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I continued. And I graduated City College with a degree in electrical engineering. And then, eventually, I worked for companies that were, in those days, very exciting. They were working on missile projects already. And for me, this was like, wow. You know. And then I decided to quit and open up a business making equipment for dental laboratories. And when I quit, the chief engineer, he thought I was crazy. They were giving me raises every year. And then, when I said I'm quitting, they offered me another $1,500, which, in those days, was a lot of money. And I said, no. I got to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what I ended up doing. And I had a company manufacturing equipment for dental laboratories. And then I got-- And how old were you when you did that, when you opened that business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let's see, it was in '62, actually. So 30 years-- I was 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, married. And, and my older daughter was just born that summer when I started. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have, have three children. My older daughter, Paulette, and she's-- she met her husband in medical school. So he's a doctor, and she's a psychiatrist. And then the middle one is Kenneth Ginsburg, Kenny. And he's married, and he has two children. And he works in the finance area for-- He used to work for Kidder, Peabody. Now he works for a Canadian securities company. And they already made him vice president. So he's doing all right. And my younger daughter, Suzie, Suzanne, she graduated Cornell and found out that the Japanese are interviewing for jobs in Japan to teach English. So she took that interview, and she was accepted. So she spent a year teaching English. And then she got a job at the University of Kyoto to do some research with this professor for a year, but he made her stay another year. And she just got back a few months ago. In fact, she's going to come the 22nd, tomorrow. She's coming in from San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still in the dental equipment business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm still in the dental equipment business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, before your wife comes in, I just want to ask you, is there anything that you would like to add? Or is there any message you would like to say, briefly, on this, the 50th anniversary of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's very little, really, you can say. If you look at Bosnia-- and, you know, my area was like a little Bosnia. And it's still happening 50 years later. And if you look at Rwanda, you know, the Tutsis and the whatnot killing each other by the hundreds of thousands, by the millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there is something, maybe, in the genes of people, that they kill each other. I wish it wasn't like so, you know. But that's a problem. And at sometime in the future, if your grandchildren look at this tape, what would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll-- if they don't know it themselves by now, I'll, I'll, I'll tell them that this was a dark chapter in our history. And, and no way can Europe be the same after what happened, whether it's the religious community or the government communities. I once spoke in a little town in Garrison, New York. And it's mostly non-Jews there. And people were very receptive, very nice. And one of them asked me a question, somehow trying to relate with the Vatican. And I said, well, I usually-- I don't like to get involved with-- but, I said to him, Poland was such a religious country. Probably 95% or more of the Polish Catholics were very religious. And if at that time, the Vatican or the pope would have sent a message, at least to the Polish priests or the cardinals-- it didn't have to be in writing, even, if you are afraid to do it in writing because that could be dangerous-- to say to them, hey, you know, this is-- what's happening now is much more than, you know, than we normally allow with whatever antisemitism. Unfortunately, Poland was an antisemitic country. And it wasn't just by themselves. They learned it from others. I would say thousands, maybe hundreds of thousands of Jews in Poland would have survived because the Germans, a lot of times, didn't know who was Jewish and who was not Jewish. But the local population made sure to point out, in case the Germans missed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Betty. Her maiden name is Hellner, H-E-L-L-N-E-R. She survived the war with her parents and brother. And she was about three years old, so she doesn't remember that much. What do you remember from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't have any recollection at all of what transpired during the war. Only what my parents told me or my older brother has told me. And we were very lucky in the fact that we had many Christian friends in the village who were-- couldn't help us outwardly, but they did help us indirectly by not giving us away and leaving food for us. And, and of course, without my mother and father, I wouldn't have made it. I don't have any recollections. That's why I'm not as active as my husband is. And I find that it's very important to, to have a record of it. And it's important that they have people like my husband that is doing it. So I'm sort of in the background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. This picture shows a group of children in Hebrew school, in kindergarten, and in the bottom row, sixth from the right, is my sister. And this was taken before the war. The second picture shows my parents with a group of friends. And this was also taken before the war. My mother is middle of row, third from right. And my father is bottom row, first from left. And this picture shows dedication of that Hebrew school where the children were photographed. And my father is holding a dedication tray during the program. And the bottom is an enlargement of the picture from the kindergarten. And it shows my sister, second from right-- from left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And around when was that? Doesn't matter-- kindergarten's good. Are you OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding picture of my oldest daughter, Paulette. And she's married to Alan Wolf. They're both doctors. They met in medical school. And this is my son, Kenneth Ginsburg. And he's married to Vicki [INAUDIBLE]. She originally is from St. Petersburg, Russia. And he now works in the finance industry. This is my youngest daughter, Suzanne Ginsburg. She just spent three years in Japan doing research and teaching English. And now she's back in the United States, in San Francisco. And these are my grandchildren. On the bottom-- on the bottom is Zachary. And next to him is Olivia. And on top of Zachary is Ari. And those are my daughter's three children. And on the top left is Paulina and Sasha, and those are my son's children. In 1993, I went back to my hometown. And some of the surviving people collected some money, and we put up three monuments on three mass graves. The top one has 400 men that were killed by the Einsatzgruppen in the beginning, when they first arrived in our town. The middle one is the mass grave when they killed everybody in town. And that one contains 4,500 people, including, including my mother, my sister, my grandparents, and many relatives. And the bottom is a grave that contains some of the people that managed to run away from our town during the killing. They were tracked down later by Ukrainians and Germans. And they were buried in the woods. And that grave was left open for one month while they were capturing those people. Even in our area, and in our town where the population was very unfriendly and collaborating with the Germans, there was still a few people that, in a way, risked their life to help Jews. And this woman is one of them that saved a couple that now live in, in Florida. His name is Yakov Biber. And she was honored by Yad Vashem. And her name is inscribed in the Washington Holocaust Museum and, and the-- on a special wall of righteous people.
